subsections.label: Intuition First
subsections.heading: The Need for Super Shortcuts
subsections.body: In the previous chapter, we found derivatives using the formal limit definition. While mathematically robust, doing this for complex equations is tedious. Think of the limit definition as walking to a destination; the rules of differentiation are the cars, trains, and planes that get you there infinitely faster. These rules are patterns we notice every time we take a limit — once spotted, they let us bypass the limit entirely.

analogy.title: The Gear Analogy (Chain Rule) & Teamwork Analogy (Product/Quotient Rule)
analogy.body:
Chain Rule — Interlocking Gears: If gear A turns twice as fast as gear B, and gear B turns three times as fast as gear C, gear A turns 2×3=6 times as fast as gear C. You multiply the rates. When a function is nested inside another (like an onion), you cannot just differentiate the outside — you multiply the rate of the outer layer by the rate of the inner layer.

Product Rule — Company Revenue: If your revenue = (units sold) × (price per item), and both are changing simultaneously, you cannot just multiply the two rates. The Product Rule tells you exactly how to handle this.
keyPoints: Power Rule: d/dx(xⁿ) = nxⁿ⁻¹ — bring the exponent down, subtract one.
Chain Rule: d/dx[f(g(x))] = f'(g(x)) · g'(x) — outer derivative times inner derivative.
Product Rule: (uv)' = u'v + uv' — first times deriv of second plus second times deriv of first.
Quotient Rule: (u/v)' = (vu' - uv') / v² — lo d-hi minus hi d-lo over lo squared.

definitions.definition: d/dx(c) = 0. The derivative of any constant is zero — a constant never changes, so its rate of change is zero.
d/dx[c·f(x)] = c·f'(x). A constant multiplier just carries through differentiation unchanged.
d/dx[f(x) ± g(x)] = f'(x) ± g'(x). Derivatives distribute over addition and subtraction.
d/dx(xⁿ) = n·xⁿ⁻¹ for any real n. Bring the exponent down as a multiplier and reduce the exponent by one. Works for negative and fractional exponents too.
If y = u(x)·v(x), then y' = u'v + uv'. First times derivative of second, plus second times derivative of first.
If y = u(x)/v(x), then y' = (vu' - uv') / v². Derivative of top times bottom minus derivative of bottom times top, all over bottom squared.
If y = f(g(x)), then y' = f'(g(x)) · g'(x). Differentiate the outer function (leaving inner untouched) then multiply by the derivative of the inner function.
= cos x. The derivative of sine is cosine.
= -sin x. The derivative of cosine is negative sine.
= eˣ. The exponential function is its own derivative — the unique function that grows proportionally to itself.
= 1/x. The derivative of the natural log is the reciprocal.
The derivative of the derivative. f''(x) > 0 means concave up (holds water). f''(x) < 0 means concave down (sheds water). Used to classify critical points as maxima or minima.

examples.title: Power Rule + Sum Rule on a Polynomial
steps: Apply the Sum Rule: differentiate term by term from left to right.

examples.title: Quotient Rule
steps: Identify top u(x) = x² - 4x and bottom v(x) = x + 2.

subsections.label: Visual
subsections.heading: Visual Understanding
visualNotes: Concavity: Where f''(x) > 0 the curve is concave up (cups water). Where f''(x) < 0 it is concave down (sheds water). The inflection point is where concavity flips.
Optimization: At a maximum or minimum, the tangent line is perfectly flat — slope = 0. Setting f'(x) = 0 locates these peaks and valleys precisely.
Power Rule geometrically: The area of a square with side x is x². A tiny increase dx adds two thin strips of area x·dx each, giving a total rate 2x — exactly what the power rule says.

snippets.title: Basic Differentiation Rules
snippets.code:
from sympy import symbols, diff, sqrt, cos, exp, sin

x = symbols('x')

# Power Rule
f1 = x**4
print("d/dx(x^4) =", diff(f1, x))  # 4x^3

# Polynomial
p  = 2*x**4 + 7*x**3 + 6*x**2 + 2*x + 15
print("p'(x) =", diff(p, x))

# Chain Rule — trig
f2 = cos(x**2)
print("d/dx[cos(x^2)] =", diff(f2, x))  # -2x*sin(x^2)

# Chain Rule — exponential
f3 = exp(x**2)
print("d/dx[e^(x^2)] =", diff(f3, x))  # 2x*e^(x^2)

snippets.title: Higher-Order Derivatives
snippets.code:
from sympy import symbols, diff

x = symbols('x')
f = x**4

# Second derivative of x^4
print("f''(x)  =", diff(f, x, 2))  # 12x^2

# Fourth derivative of x^4
print("f''''(x) =", diff(f, x, 4))  # 24

# Concavity check
g   = x**3 - 6*x**2
dg  = diff(g, x)          # 3x^2 - 12x
dg2 = diff(g, x, 2)       # 6x - 12
print("g'(x)  =", dg)
print("g''(x) =", dg2)
print("Concavity at x=3:", dg2.subs(x, 3))  # positive = concave up

snippets.title: Slope at a Point
snippets.code:
from sympy import symbols, diff

x = symbols('x')
f  = x**3 - 6*x**2

# Take the derivative
df = diff(f, x)  # 3x^2 - 12x
print("f'(x) =", df)

# Slope at x = -3
slope = df.subs(x, -3)
print("Slope at x=-3:", slope)  # 63

# Marginal cost example
C  = 25 + x**3 / 5
dC = diff(C, x)
print("Marginal cost C'(x) =", dC)          # 3x^2/5
print("Marginal cost at x=10:", dC.subs(x, 10))  # 60

questions.text: Using the Power Rule, what is d/dx(x⁷)?
questions.explanation: Power Rule: d/dx(xⁿ) = n·xⁿ⁻¹. Bring the exponent (7) down as a multiplier and subtract 1 from the exponent: 7x⁷⁻¹ = 7x⁶.

The exponential function eˣ is its own derivative — it is the unique function that grows proportionally to itself. Never apply the Power Rule to eˣ; the Power Rule is only for variable bases with constant exponents.

questions.text: To differentiate f(x) = (x² + 1)¹⁰, which rule do you need?
questions.explanation: There is a function (x²+1) nested inside a power function — the classic case for the Chain Rule. Result: 10(x²+1)⁹ · 2x = 20x(x²+1)⁹. Forgetting the ·2x is the most common mistake.

questions.text: If f''(x) < 0 at a point x₀, what can you say about the graph of f at that point?
options: f has a local minimum at x₀
f is concave down at x₀
questions.explanation: The second derivative describes concavity. f''(x) < 0 means concave down — like an umbrella or a hill. f''(x) > 0 means concave up — like a cup or a valley.

questions.text: The Quotient Rule formula for d/dx[u/v] is:
questions.explanation: Quotient Rule: (vu' - uv') / v². Remember: derivative of top times bottom, minus derivative of bottom times top, all over bottom squared. The order matters — swapping gives the wrong sign.

problems.label: Concept Check
problems.hint: Apply the Power Rule to each term separately using the Sum Rule.

problems.label: Application
problems.problem: Using the Product Rule, find y' for y = (x² + 4x - 11)(6x³ - √x).
problems.hint: Let u = x² + 4x - 11 and v = 6x³ - x^(1/2). Find u', v', then apply y' = u'v + uv'.

problems.label: Thinking-Based
problems.problem: Differentiate f(x) = cos((4x)²) using the Chain Rule.
problems.hint: First simplify (4x)² = 16x². Now you have cos(16x²) — two layers: cosine is outer, 16x² is inner.
problems.solution:
Simplify: f(x) = cos(16x²).
Outer: cos(·) → derivative is -sin(·).
Inner: 16x² → derivative is 32x.
Chain Rule: f'(x) = -sin(16x²) · 32x = -32x·sin(16x²)

problems.label: Economics
problems.problem: The total cost to produce x units is C(x) = 25 + (1/5)x³. Find the marginal cost and evaluate it at the 10th unit.
problems.hint: Marginal cost = C'(x). Differentiate then substitute x = 10.
problems.solution:
C'(x) = 0 + (1/5)·3x² = (3/5)x².
At x = 10: C'(10) = (3/5)(100) = 60.
The cost to produce one additional unit at the 10th unit is $60.

mistakes.title: The Fraction Trap
mistakes.description: A massive pitfall is differentiating u/v by simply computing u'/v'. This is mathematically illegal. You MUST use the Quotient Rule: (vu' - uv') / v². The derivative of a fraction is NOT the fraction of the derivatives.

mistakes.title: Applying Power Rule to Exponentials
mistakes.description: Students apply nxⁿ⁻¹ to eˣ, getting x·eˣ⁻¹. Wrong. The Power Rule only works when the BASE is variable and the EXPONENT is constant (like x²). When the base is constant and exponent is variable (like eˣ), the derivative is uniquely eˣ — it is its own derivative.

mistakes.title: Forgetting the Inner Derivative (Chain Rule)
mistakes.description: When differentiating (x²+1)¹⁰, students write 10(x²+1)⁹ and stop. They forget to multiply by the derivative of the inner function (2x). The correct answer is 20x(x²+1)⁹. Never skip the inner derivative — it is half of the Chain Rule.

insights.title: Implicit Differentiation
insights.body: When a function is mixed (like x² + y² = 3), you cannot isolate y easily. Implicit differentiation takes the derivative of both sides with respect to x. When differentiating a y term, treat y as a nested function and apply the Chain Rule — multiply by dy/dx. Example: 2x + 2y·y' = 0 → y' = -x/y. This works for circles, ellipses, and any curve that fails the vertical line test.

insights.title: Taylor's Theorem (Infinite Polynomials)
insights.body: The rules of differentiation allow us to approximate impossibly complex functions (like sin x or eˣ) as simple infinite polynomials. By matching all higher-order derivatives at a point x=0, we build a Maclaurin Series. For example: eˣ = 1 + x + x²/2! + x³/3! + … This perfectly calculates exponential growth using only basic arithmetic — and it is why engineers can compute sin and cos on a chip.

applications.description: Position s(t) → velocity s'(t) → acceleration s''(t). Using the Chain Rule, physicists solve related rates problems: if you know how the volume of water in a tank changes, the Chain Rule tells you how fast the height changes.

The Chain Rule IS backpropagation. Training a neural network requires computing how the error changes with respect to each weight — a chain of derivatives applied iteratively through every layer. Without the Chain Rule, gradient descent and all of modern AI is impossible.

Marginal cost and marginal revenue are derivatives of their respective functions. Setting the derivative of profit to zero finds the optimal production level. Elasticity of demand is also a derivative — how much does demand change per unit change in price?

When equations are too complex to solve algebraically, Newton's Method uses the tangent line (derivative) iteratively. Starting from a guess x₀, the next better guess is x₁ = x₀ - f(x₀)/f'(x₀). This converges to a root extremely quickly and is built into most root-finding software.
